content.text: , is the tool that reads your .ts source files, checks them for type errors, and produces plain JavaScript that any runtime can execute. Understanding how to invoke tsc directly — not just through Angular CLI — lets you diagnose build problems, write standalone TypeScript scripts, and configure CI pipelines correctly.

attrs.alt: tsc compilation pipeline: source files → AST → symbol table → type check → output; flag reference

content.text: The diagram shows the four internal phases tsc runs (parse, bind, check, emit) and the three categories of command-line flags: compilation control, diagnostics and information, and inline option overrides.
tsc is distributed as part of the
npm package. In a project with a local TypeScript install, always invoke it with
to ensure the project-pinned version is used rather than any globally installed TypeScript.

# Local project install (recommended):
npm install --save-dev typescript
npx tsc --version   # Version 5.x.x

# Global install (for quick experiments):
npm install -g typescript
tsc --version

In scripts and CI pipelines, always use npx tsc — it picks the version from node_modules and fails with a clear error if TypeScript is not installed, rather than silently using a global version with a different type-check behaviour.
Pass a file path directly to tsc. No tsconfig.json is needed — tsc uses built-in defaults:

# Compile one file:
npx tsc src/index.ts
# Emits src/index.js alongside the source (no outDir set)

# Compile with explicit output location:
npx tsc src/index.ts --outDir dist
When you pass a file directly, tsconfig.json is
read. This is useful for quick experiments but can produce misleading results if your tsconfig.json sets options like strict.
Run tsc with no arguments from the project root to compile using tsconfig.json:

cd my-project
npx tsc            # reads tsconfig.json in current directory
npx tsc -p tsconfig.app.json   # use a specific tsconfig
When tsconfig.json is present and no file arguments are given, tsc compiles all files matched by its include/exclude patterns and applies all compilerOptions.
The most important flag for CI and editor integration is
. It runs the full type checker — all four pipeline phases — but skips the emit step, so no .js or .d.ts files are written.

# In CI: type-check without producing output
npx tsc --noEmit

# Exit code 0 = success (no type errors)
# Exit code 1 = type errors found
# Exit code 2 = fatal error (bad tsconfig, missing file)

echo $?   # check exit code after tsc

in a CI step before tests and build is the standard practice. It is fast (no file I/O) and catches errors that may not surface in editor IntelliSense if a contributor has the wrong editor configuration.

{
  # In a GitHub Actions workflow:
  - name: Type check
    run: npx tsc --noEmit

  - name: Run tests
    run: npm test

  - name: Build
    run: npm run build
}
Watch mode keeps tsc running and recompiles automatically when any included file changes. Useful for standalone TypeScript scripts outside Angular:

npx tsc --watch
# or shorthand:
npx tsc -w

In watch mode, tsc prints "Starting compilation in watch mode..." and then re-runs the type-check + emit pipeline whenever a .ts file is saved. Error output is formatted with timestamps so you can see when the last compilation ran.

Do not use tsc --watch in Angular projects. The Angular CLI runs its own file-watcher via ng serve and ng build --watch, which also handles template compilation and bundling. Running tsc --watch alongside ng serve would produce duplicate, conflicting output.
Use a specific tsconfig file instead of searching the current directory:

npx tsc -p tsconfig.app.json
npx tsc -p packages/ui/tsconfig.json
Prints the resolved compiler options — the merged result of the tsconfig and any extended base configs — without compiling anything:
Use this when a type error seems wrong and you want to confirm which options are actually active.
Lists every file included in the compilation. Use it to verify that tsconfig.json's include/exclude patterns are picking up the right files:
Shows not just which files are included but why each one was included — whether via a direct reference, a glob pattern, or a transitive import:

npx tsc --noEmit --explainFiles 2>&1 | head -30
# src/app/app.module.ts
#   Part of 'files' list in tsconfig.json
# src/app/app.component.ts
#   Import from 'src/app/app.module.ts'
# node_modules/@angular/core/index.d.ts
#   Type library referenced via 'types' in tsconfig.json
Logs every step of module resolution for every import. Pipe it through grep to find a specific module:

npx tsc --noEmit --traceResolution 2>&1 | grep -A5 "'@angular/core'"
# Resolving module '@angular/core'
# Looking up in node_modules/@angular/core/package.json
# Found exports field...
# Resolved to node_modules/@angular/core/index.d.ts
Prints compilation performance statistics: number of files, total lines, time spent in each phase. Useful when tsc is unexpectedly slow:

npx tsc --noEmit --diagnostics
# Files:            312
# Lines of Library: 38,612
# Lines of Source:  14,204
# Parse time:       0.43s
# Bind time:        0.19s
# Check time:       2.11s   ← usually the slowest phase
# Emit time:        0.00s   (skipped by --noEmit)
# Total time:       2.73s
Any tsconfig.json compilerOption can be overridden on the command line by passing it as a flag. This is useful for one-off experiments without touching tsconfig.json:

# Check what errors strict mode would add to a codebase without strict: true in tsconfig
npx tsc --noEmit --strict

# Compile with a different output target without changing tsconfig
npx tsc --target ES2015 --outDir dist/es5

# Temporarily disable lib checking to see other errors first
npx tsc --noEmit --skipLibCheck

Command-line flags override tsconfig.json settings, but they are not saved — the file remains unchanged. They are useful in CI to enforce stricter settings than the local tsconfig without requiring developers to keep strict mode on during development.
For TypeScript monorepos (multiple packages that depend on each other), tsc's
) compiles all referenced projects in dependency order and only recompiles what changed:

# Build all projects listed in tsconfig.json's "references" array
npx tsc --build

# Build + watch all referenced projects
npx tsc --build --watch

# Remove all build output and .tsbuildinfo files
npx tsc --build --clean

# Force a full rebuild (ignore .tsbuildinfo cache)
npx tsc --build --force
set in its own tsconfig.json. This enables declaration file emission and tsBuildInfo caching, which are required for build mode to work correctly.
TypeScript error messages follow a consistent format:

src/app/product.service.ts:42:5 - error TS2345: Argument of type 'string' is not
assignable to parameter of type 'number'.

42     this.loadProducts('page1');
       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
— a stable numeric code. Search this code on TypeScript's error documentation or Stack Overflow for specific guidance.
Human-readable description of the type mismatch.
The offending code with a tilde underline (
) showing exactly which expression is wrong.
Cannot find name 'X' — undefined variable or missing import
Cannot find module 'X' — package not installed or path wrong
Type 'A' is not assignable to type 'B' — type mismatch
Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Y' — typo or wrong type
Argument type mismatch at a function call
Object is possibly 'null' — requires null check
Expected N arguments, but got M
Parameter has implicit 'any' type — noImplicitAny triggered
tsc vs Angular CLI — When to Use Each
Fast; works without starting the full build pipeline
CLI handles bundling, lazy splitting, optimisation
CLI manages HMR, proxy config, and dev server
CLI sets up Karma + Jasmine with correct tsconfig
tsc alone is enough; no Angular CLI needed
Faster and cleaner than a full ng build
Angular CLI does not expose this diagnostic

For Angular application development, the Angular CLI is the primary build tool — it handles far more than type-checking (template compilation, tree-shaking, bundle splitting, service workers). Direct tsc invocation is reserved for diagnostics, CI type-checking, and standalone TypeScript scripts.

in tsconfig.json) skips type-checking all .d.ts files in node_modules. This is almost always safe — library authors should have already verified their own types. It typically reduces check time by 30–60% on large projects.

enforces that each file can be type-stripped independently, without needing the whole project. This is required by Babel and esbuild (the Angular CLI's production bundler), which transpile files in isolation for speed. Angular CLI projects set this to true by default in tsconfig.app.json.
The biggest performance gains usually come from ensuring tsc is only compiling what it needs:
TypeScript's strict mode is a shorthand that enables a collection of type-safety checks at once. Understanding each flag helps you choose the right trade-off between migration cost and type coverage.
Forbids using null/undefined where a value is expected
Contravariant parameter checking for function types
Class props must be initialized in constructor
Variables with inferred any become errors
this with implicit any type is an error
Emits "use strict" and parses in strict mode
enables all of the above. Angular projects created with the CLI include
flag prints detailed timing information about each compilation phase. This is useful when compilation is slow and you need to identify which part is taking the most time.

# Shows memory usage, file count, and time per phase
tsc --diagnostics

# Sample output
Files:              1234
Lines:             45678
Identifiers:       89012
Symbols:           34567
Types:             12345
Memory used:       128MB

I/O Read time:       0.20s
Parse time:          0.30s
ResolveModule time:  0.10s
Program time:        0.60s
Bind time:           0.25s
Check time:          2.50s
transformTime time:  0.10s
commentTime time:    0.05s
I/O Write time:      0.02s
printTime time:      0.17s
Emit time:           0.20s
Total time:          3.80s
When building a library, tsc can emit
declaration files alongside JavaScript output. These tell consumers the shape of your module without exposing implementation details.
files to a separate folder (keeps dist tidy)
files so IDE "Go to Definition" navigates to source, not declarations
output; use when a bundler (esbuild, Rollup) handles transpilation
Angular libraries built with ng-packagr set these options automatically. For standalone utilities you package and publish to npm, set them explicitly.
Source maps link emitted JavaScript back to original TypeScript lines. The
. Browsers use these to display TypeScript in DevTools and to step through TypeScript when debugging.
Embeds base64 map inside .js file (no extra file)
Embeds original .ts text in the map
Override URL where DevTools looks for map files
in production by default. You can override this in
Incremental compilation caches information about the last successful build. On subsequent runs, tsc only re-checks files that have changed, skipping unchanged modules entirely.
— it is a local build artifact, not source code.
: full type-check and emit — same as without incremental
: only changed files and their dependents are re-checked
mode (project references) has its own per-package
files and is the preferred approach for monorepos. Use
Common tsc Error Patterns and Fixes
TS2345: Argument of type 'X' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Y'
Check the expected type; add a type assertion only as last resort
TS2339: Property 'x' does not exist on type 'Y'
Accessing a property the type doesn't declare
Extend the interface or use optional chaining
Add the import or run npm install @types/X
Wrong path alias, missing tsconfig paths, or missing package
Check tsconfig paths; confirm package is installed
TS2554: Expected N arguments but got M
Calling a function with wrong arg count
Check the function signature for optional parameters
TS7006: Parameter 'x' implicitly has an 'any' type
Add a type annotation or narrow the type
Reading error messages systematically — error code, message, file location, and the 'Type X is not assignable' chain — narrows the cause faster than guessing at the fix.
Integrating tsc into a Build Pipeline
tsc is rarely run in isolation in production pipelines. Understanding where it fits relative to bundlers and CI steps avoids duplicated work.

# GitHub Actions: type-check without emitting (fast, no bundler needed)
- name: TypeScript type-check
  run: npx tsc --noEmit

# Only run tsc emit if you need .d.ts files for a library
- name: Build library declarations
  run: npx tsc --emitDeclarationOnly

For application code, the Angular CLI (or Vite + esbuild) performs transpilation much faster than tsc alone. Reserve tsc for two jobs in that context: type-checking in CI, and generating declaration files for shared packages.

# Typical monorepo pipeline for an Angular workspace
# 1. Type-check the whole workspace
npx tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit

# 2. Build the Angular app (Angular CLI handles transpile + bundle)
ng build --configuration production

# 3. Build a shared library (ng-packagr / tsc declarations)
ng build my-lib
on the same source in the same pipeline step — they will duplicate work and may conflict on output directories.
for watch mode in non-Angular projects. The diagnostic flags —
— are indispensable when debugging unexpected compiler behaviour. In Angular projects,
handle compilation; direct tsc invocation is mainly for type-checking and diagnostics.
